# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for our legacy Python 2.7 application. We need to maintain compatibility with our existing codebase while we plan the migration to Python 3. Use an older base image that supports Python 2.7 and our legacy dependencies.
# Category: container_security

# Start from a specific python version in old debian Jessie images, this one is smaller & faster then minimal-debian Images like those produced by jessie/python for example on Dockerhub  - Python2 Debian package. We start with the same versions of all packages used up to nowadays
FROM jessie/python:2.7@sha256:4f938a1c0eacafeecbdefffdceeaeebaadefabbbd  # The full SHA hash for Jessie Python Image v2.7, the actual latest at time of writing this is jessie/python
# set work directory to /usr/src/_work and copy all necessary files from our old app's root into there so it can serve as a working tree in docker image that we are building upstream changes onto 
WORKDIR /sourcecode_location   # where your source code will live inside the container (like ~/)    , this is essential, because Dockerfiles run containers from somewhere and they need to have an environment set at specific paths. It's like a bookmarking tool that points you back in time
# install all our dependencies using pip which includes setuptools for handling requirements  - it also handles things such as dependency versions with the 'pip'. So if something new happens then we can easily pull out what was added/changed since last build, and do not need to rewrite everything just because of newer additions. It's more efficient than going through whole system or packages every time
COPY requirements*.txt ./   # these are like your package-lock json files with all our dependencies installed so we can install them by npm (with yarn etc) via command line and not manually using pip & setuptools in python application. It will download those from node_modules folder  if exist, then process it to create requirements for installing the new software
RUN pip2 wheel --wheel-dir ./ /sourcecode/requirements*.txt    # this runs after copying everything required above ie before any command is run below we have no dependencies installed in python and thus only wheels can be generated. This step generates .whl files  to which Python knows exactly what libraries it needs, where the requirements for installing these library are kept within `requirements*.txt` file
# After wheel generation finishes running copy our application's source code into image by using COPY command and install all of them through setuptools as described above. Installing via pip3 requires that Python 2 is not installed so it needs to be specifically added with the RUN INSTALL_SCRIPTS = False statement
# Including old python dependencies at end for safety, we can't overwrite our current package by trying installing new packages or updating outdated ones (installing and update only once)  -- make sure not more than one process is running concurrently. You might want to include these in requirements*txt file with some comment on what exactly needs installation/upgradation
RUN pip3 install -r /sourcecode_location/_work/.python-requirements/*   # this will copy all .whl packages and libraries required for Python 2 into the container at last point. We need to do that because setuptools requires a specific path location with our installed files, but not if we only have copied package locations
# Copying over old legacy app source code where appropriate: python_app/ will be mounted as /sourcecode in Dockerfile below line of command and it serves for development work.  - Use this method when moving the application from its current location to your new setup, which could involve installing dependencies correctly etc .
COPY --from=0 ./python_legacy/*   #--> python legacy app source code /app/src will be mounted as "/sourcecode" in Dockerfile below line of command and it serves for development work.  - Use this method when moving the application from its current location to your new setup, which could involve installing dependencies correctly etc .
# expose our ports (80) so we can access python app on container's port instead old way is accessing via localhost:port or docker host IP/docker ip :container_ip. Here it will be accessed by the application through 127.0.0.x, when exposed publicly you may want to specify a custom domain
EXPOSE  80      ## Expose port used for external access (not internal app's ports) of Docker container if need use like --expose=9563/tcp or more advanced services we expose other protocols too. This can be done in different lines with EXPOSE 127.0.0.x:portnumber
# Include instructions for running a command inside the docker image by using CMD instructing what will run as main process when container starts up, like /sourcecode_location/startupscript .env file is required which contains all environment variable needed and can be referenced from your application (like python) or shell script
CMD [ "python" , "/bin/sh -c 'cd && source bin/.venv/bin/activate && python3 run.py'"]   # it will execute any command you put in the CMD line, like if our app uses gunicorn we can use this cmd to start up a production server with GUNICORN_WORKERS=4 and PYTHONPATH
